title: Unveiling Magnetic Frustration via the Elastocaloric Effect
authors: Eric C. Andrade, Pedro M. Cônsoli, Matthias Vojta
abstract: Motivated by experimental progress in pressure and strain tuning of quantum materials, we examine the thermodynamic response of frustrated magnets to uniaxial strain. Specifically, we study Ising and Heisenberg models on spatially anisotropic triangular (and, for the Ising model, also kagome) lattices. We determine the entropy as a function of temperature and strain, and use it to compute the elastic Grüneisen ratio $η$. The Ising models can be strain-tuned into and out of classical spin-liquid phases, and we show that $η$ can become arbitrarily large at low temperature $T$ near the point of maximal frustration, a universal hallmark of an extensive ground-state entropy. In contrast, the spin-$1/2$ Heisenberg model is moderately frustrated and displays multiple $T=0$ phase transitions. These transitions dominate $η$ at low $T$ while the intermediate-$T$ behavior is similar to that of the Ising model. We discuss the extent to which the elastic Grüneisen ratio can be used to deduce the phase diagram, and we connect our results to recent experiments on triangular-lattice magnets.
summary: This paper explores how uniaxial strain affects the thermodynamics of frustrated magnets using the elastocaloric effect ($\eta$). By analyzing Ising and Heisenberg models on triangular and kagome lattices, the authors show that the divergence of $\eta$ at low temperatures provides a universal signature for extensive ground-state entropy. This work offers a powerful theoretical tool to probe magnetic frustration in quantum materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the thermodynamic response of frustrated magnets to uniaxial strain to reveal the underlying physics of magnetic frustration.
main_result: For Ising models, the elastic Grüneisen ratio ($\eta$) diverges arbitrarily at low temperatures near maximal frustration, serving as a universal hallmark of extensive ground-state entropy.
method: The authors calculate the entropy ($s$) and subsequently the elastic Grüneisen ratio ($\eta$) using theoretical models and analytical expansions.
model_or_system: The work analyzes Ising and Heisenberg models on spatially anisotropic triangular and kagome lattices. The Hamiltonian is modified by uniaxial strain ($\epsilon$), which linearly alters the exchange couplings ($J$ and $J'$).
key_observables: Elastic Grüneisen ratio ($\eta$), entropy ($s$), and specific heat ($c$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), uniaxial strain ($\epsilon$), and the ratio of exchange couplings ($J'/J$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on analytical expansions for $c$ and $s$ in terms of $\epsilon$, and comparisons are made between different temperature and strain limits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the specific heat, entropy, and the elastocaloric effect ($\eta$) for the Ising model on the anisotropic triangular lattice, showing enhancement of $\eta$ as $|\epsilon| 	o 0$ at low $T$.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the Ising and Heisenberg models, deriving $\eta$ from the strain dependence of entropy. It contrasts the behavior of the two models, highlighting the role of ground-state entropy in the divergence of $\eta$ for the Ising case.
why_interesting: The connection between macroscopic thermodynamic responses (like the elastocaloric effect) and microscopic quantum properties (like ground-state degeneracy) is highly relevant for understanding quantum phase transitions in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Spatially anisotropic triangular lattice, with exchange coupling J along two directions (full lines) and J′

Figure 2. Elastocaloric effect η for the anisotropic triangular- lattice Ising model as a function of temperature T. We employ the parametrization for the exchange constant as in Eq. (4). (a) η as a function of T for α = 0.5 and several values of the strain ϵ. (b) η as a function of T for |ϵ| = 0.05 and several values of α. The full (dashed) curves correspond to ϵ > (<)0.

Figure 4. Elastocaloric effect η for the anisotropic triangular- lattice Ising model as a function of temperature T and uniax- ial strain ϵ, computed with α = 0.5. The color code indicates the value of η. We impose cutoffs η = ±20J0 for the sake of presentation. The inset shows cuts η(ϵ) at low T, illustrating the non-commuting limits. The points represent the exact computation of η, while the lines depict the low-ϵ expansion around the isotropic point, as discussed in the text.

Figure 3. Strain dependence of (a) specific heat c and (b) entropy per site s at low temperatures T for the anisotropic triangular-lattice Ising model, computed with α = 0.5. The singular behavior at finite positive ϵ arises from the thermal phase transition. The color coding is the same for both fig- ures.

Figure 5. Thermodynamic observables computed by ED of the spin-1/2 Heisenberg model on the anisotropic triangular lattice. The left and right columns show data for anisotropy parameters α = 0.5 and 1.0, respectively. (a,b) Entropy per site, s, as a function of temperature T and uniaxial strain ϵ. The white curves correspond to s = 0.03, 0.1, 0.2, . . . , 0.6. (c,d) Gr¨uneisen ratio η, showing signatures of two quantum phase transitions at low T and a single characteristic sign change for T/J0 ≳0.5. The data for T/J0 ≤0.05 are omitted due to finite-size effects.
